content:
# Genesis Chapter 28 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 15v–16r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_028_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_028_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 24–25 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0028 | https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0029

---

*Isaac Blesses Jacob and Sends Him to Paddan-aram (vv. 1–5)*

**28:1** And Isaac called [וַיִּקְרָא יִצְחָק] to Jacob [אֶל-יַעֲקֹב] and blessed him [וַיְבָרֶךְ אֹתוֹ] and commanded him [וַיְצַוֵּהוּ] and said to him [וַיֹּאמֶר לוֹ]: You shall not take [לֹא-תִקַּח] a wife [אִשָּׁה] from the daughters of [מִבְּנוֹת] Canaan [כְּנָעַן].

**28:2** Rise [קוּם] — go [לֵךְ] to Paddan-aram [פַּדֶּנָה אֲרָם] — to the house of [בֵּית] Bethuel [בְּתוּאֵל] your mother's [אִמֶּךָ] father [אֲבִי] — and take [וְקַח-לְךָ] from there [מִשָּׁם] a wife [אִשָּׁה] from the daughters of [מִבְּנוֹת] Laban [לָבָן] your mother's [אִמֶּךָ] brother [אֲחִי].

**28:3** And El Shaddai [וְאֵל שַׁדַּי] — may he bless you [יְבָרֵךְ אֹתְךָ] and make you fruitful [וְיַפְרְךָ] and make you numerous [וְיַרְבֶּךָ] — and you shall become [וְהָיִיתָ] a congregation of [לִקְהַל] peoples [עַמִּים].

**28:4** And may he give you [וְיִתֶּן-לְךָ] the blessing of [אֶת-בִּרְכַּת] Abraham [אַבְרָהָם] — to you [לְךָ] and to your seed [וּלְזַרְעֲךָ] with you [אִתָּךְ] — to cause you to possess [לְרִשְׁתְּךָ] the land of [אֶת-אֶרֶץ] your sojournings [מְגֻרֶיךָ] which [אֲשֶׁר] God gave [נָתַן אֱלֹהִים] to Abraham [לְאַבְרָהָם].

**28:5** And Isaac sent Jacob away [וַיִּשְׁלַח יִצְחָק אֶת-יַעֲקֹב] and he went [וַיֵּלֶךְ] to Paddan-aram [פַּדֶּנָה אֲרָם] — to Laban [אֶל-לָבָן] son of [בֶּן] Bethuel [בְּתוּאֵל] the Aramean [הָאֲרַמִּי] — brother of [אֲחִי] Rebekah [רִבְקָה] — mother of [אֵם] Jacob [יַעֲקֹב] and Esau [וְעֵשָׂו].

---

*Esau Takes a Third Wife — Ishmael's Daughter (vv. 6–9)*

**28:6** And Esau saw [וַיַּרְא עֵשָׂו] that Isaac [כִּי-בֵרַךְ יִצְחָק] had blessed [אֶת-יַעֲקֹב] Jacob [אֶת-יַעֲקֹב] and sent him [וַיְשַׁלַּח אֹתוֹ] to Paddan-aram [פַּדֶּנָה אֲרָם] to take [לָקַחַת] for himself from there [לוֹ מִשָּׁם] a wife [אִשָּׁה] in his blessing him [בְּבָרְכוֹ אֹתוֹ] — and commanded him [וַיְצַוֵּהוּ] saying [לֵאמֹר]: You shall not take [לֹא-תִקַּח] a wife [אִשָּׁה] from the daughters of [מִבְּנוֹת] Canaan [כְּנָעַן] —

**28:7** and Jacob listened [וַיִּשְׁמַע יַעֲקֹב] to his father [אֶל-אָבִיו] and to his mother [וְאֶל-אִמּוֹ] and went [וַיֵּלֶךְ] to Paddan-aram [פַּדֶּנָה אֲרָם] —

**28:8** and Esau saw [וַיַּרְא עֵשָׂו] that [כִּי] evil [רָעוֹת] were the daughters of [בְּנוֹת] Canaan [כְּנָעַן] in the eyes of [בְּעֵינֵי] Isaac [יִצְחָק] his father [אָבִיו].

**28:9** And Esau went [וַיֵּלֶךְ עֵשָׂו] to Ishmael [אֶל-יִשְׁמָעֵאל] and took [וַיִּקַּח] — besides [עַל] the wives [נָשָׁיו] that he had [אֲשֶׁר-לוֹ] — Mahalath [אֶת-מָחֲלַת] the daughter of [בַּת] Ishmael [יִשְׁמָעֵאל] son of [בֶּן] Abraham [אַבְרָהָם] — sister of [אֲחוֹת] Nebaioth [נְבָיוֹת] — for himself as a wife [לוֹ לְאִשָּׁה].

---

*Jacob Departs — The Dream at Bethel (vv. 10–15)*

**28:10** And Jacob went out [וַיֵּצֵא יַעֲקֹב] from Beer-sheba [מִבְּאֵר שָׁבַע] and went [וַיֵּלֶךְ] toward Haran [חָרָנָה].

**28:11** And he came upon [וַיִּפְגַּע] a [certain] place [בַּמָּקוֹם] and spent the night there [וַיָּלֶן שָׁם] for the sun had set [כִּי-בָא הַשֶּׁמֶשׁ] — and he took [וַיִּקַּח] from the stones of [מֵאַבְנֵי] the place [הַמָּקוֹם] and put [וַיָּשֶׂם] [them as] his head-rest [מְרַאֲשֹׁתָיו] — and lay down [וַיִּשְׁכַּב] in that [בַּ] place [מָּקוֹם הַהוּא].

**28:12** And he dreamed [וַיַּחֲלֹם] — and behold [וְהִנֵּה] — a sullam [סֻלָּם, a stairway/ramp] set [מֻצָּב] earthward [אַרְצָה] with its top [וְרֹאשׁוֹ] reaching [מַגִּיעַ] to the heavens [הַשָּׁמָיְמָה] — and behold [וְהִנֵּה] — messengers of God [מַלְאֲכֵי אֱלֹהִים] ascending [עֹלִים] and descending [וְיֹרְדִים] on it [בּוֹ].

**28:13** And behold [וְהִנֵּה] — the LORD [יְהוָה] stood above it ['alav — עָלָיו, upon it / beside him] — and said [וַיֹּאמַר]: I am [אֲנִי] the LORD [יְהוָה] — the God of [אֱלֹהֵי] Abraham [אַבְרָהָם] your father [אָבִיךָ] and the God of [אֱלֹהֵי] Isaac [יִצְחָק] — the land [הָאָרֶץ] upon which [אֲשֶׁר] you are lying [אַתָּה שֹׁכֵב עָלֶיהָ] — to you [לְךָ] I will give it [אֶתְּנֶנָּה] and to your seed [וּלְזַרְעֶךָ].

**28:14** And your seed shall be [וְהָיָה זַרְעֲךָ] as the dust of [כַּעֲפַר] the earth [הָאָרֶץ] and you shall spread forth [וּפָרַצְתָּ] westward [יָמָּה] and eastward [וָקֵדְמָה] and northward [וְצָפֹנָה] and southward [וְנֶגְבָּה] — and in you [וּבְךָ] shall bless themselves [וְנִבְרְכוּ] all the families of [כָּל-מִשְׁפְּחֹת] the earth [הָאֲדָמָה] — and in your seed [וּבְזַרְעֶךָ].

**28:15** And behold [וְהִנֵּה] — I am with you [אָנֹכִי עִמָּךְ] and I will keep you [וּשְׁמַרְתִּיךָ] in all [בְּכֹל] [the places] where you go [אֲשֶׁר תֵּלֵךְ] and I will bring you back [וַהֲשִׁבֹתִיךָ] to this [הַ] land [אֲדָמָה הַזֹּאת] for I will not leave you [כִּי לֹא אֶעֱזָבְךָ] until [עַד] I have done [אֲשֶׁר עָשִׂיתִי] what I have spoken to you [אֵת אֲשֶׁר-דִּבַּרְתִּי לָךְ].

---

*Jacob Wakes — His Vow at Bethel (vv. 16–22)*

**28:16** And Jacob awoke [וַיִּיקַץ יַעֲקֹב] from his sleep [מִשְּׁנָתוֹ] and said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Surely [אָכֵן] the LORD [יְהוָה] is in this [הַ] place [מָּקוֹם הַזֶּה] and I [וְאָנֹכִי] — I did not know [לֹא יָדַעְתִּי].

**28:17** And he was afraid [וַיִּירָא] and said [וַיֹּאמַר]: How awesome [מַה-נּוֹרָא] is this [הַ] place [מָּקוֹם הַזֶּה]! This is nothing other than [אֵין זֶה כִּי אִם] the house of God [בֵּית אֱלֹהִים] — and this [וְזֶה] is the gate of [שַׁעַר] the heavens [הַשָּׁמָיִם].

**28:18** And Jacob rose early [וַיַּשְׁכֵּם יַעֲקֹב] in the morning [בַּבֹּקֶר] and took [וַיִּקַּח] the stone [אֶת-הָאֶבֶן] that he had put [אֲשֶׁר-שָׂם] as his head-rest [מְרַאֲשֹׁתָיו] and set it up [וַיָּשֶׂם אֹתָהּ] as a massebah [מַצֵּבָה, a standing-stone/pillar] and poured [וַיִּצֹק] oil [שֶׁמֶן] upon its top [עַל-רֹאשָׁהּ].

**28:19** And he called [וַיִּקְרָא] the name of [אֶת-שֵׁם] that [הַ] place [מָּקוֹם הַהוּא] Bethel [בֵּית-אֵל, "house of God"] — but the name of [וְאוּלָם שֵׁם] the city [הָעִיר] had formerly [לְרִאשֹׁנָה] been Luz [לוּז].

**28:20** And Jacob vowed [וַיִּדַּר יַעֲקֹב] a vow [נֶדֶר] saying [לֵאמֹר]: If [אִם] God [אֱלֹהִים] will be [יִהְיֶה] with me [עִמָּדִי] and will keep me [וּשְׁמָרַנִי] in this [הַ] way [דֶּרֶךְ הַזֶּה] that I am going [אֲשֶׁר אָנֹכִי הוֹלֵךְ] and will give me [וְנָתַן-לִי] bread [לֶחֶם] to eat [לֶאֱכֹל] and clothing [וּבֶגֶד] to wear [לִלְבֹּשׁ] —

**28:21** and I return [וְשַׁבְתִּי] in peace [בְשָׁלוֹם] to the house of [אֶל-בֵּית] my father [אָבִי] — and the LORD [וַיהוָה] shall be [הָיָה] my God [לִי לֵאלֹהִים] —

**28:22** and this stone [וְהָאֶבֶן הַזֹּאת] that I have set up [אֲשֶׁר-שַׂמְתִּי] as a massebah [מַצֵּבָה] shall be [יִהְיֶה] the house of God [בֵּית אֱלֹהִים] — and all [וְכֹל] that you give me [אֲשֶׁר תִּתֶּן-לִי] — tithing I will tithe it [עַשֵּׂר אֲעַשְּׂרֶנּוּ] to you [לָךְ].

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

**On reading the Leningrad Codex, Folios 15v–16r:**

LC page 1 (folio 15v) is photographed inverted (rotated 180°) — consistent with how this folio was captured in the West Semitic Research Project archive, as noted for Genesis 27. Once mentally re-oriented to standard reading position, the three-column Sephardic square hand is fully legible with complete vocalization and cantillation (te'amim). The right column (reading right to left in Hebrew, but the rightmost column on the page) carries the end of Genesis 27:43–46 transitioning into Genesis 28:1. The center and left columns carry Genesis 28:1 through approximately 28:15. The parashah boundary between Genesis 27 and 28 is marked with a פ (parashah petuchah — open section) marker visible at the section break before 28:1, correctly demarcating the new narrative unit beginning with Isaac's final instructions to Jacob. Masorah parva notations are legible in the margins, with masorah magna at the top and bottom of the page (inverted with the image). The cantillation marks throughout the ladder-dream passage (vv. 12–15) are intact and legible — the tiphcha, etnahta, and sof pasuq markers on key verses are all clearly visible.

LC page 2 (folio 16r) is in standard photographic orientation. Three columns carry Genesis 28:16–22 through the beginning of Genesis 29. The waking scene (vv. 16–17), the massebah-setting (v. 18), the Bethel naming (v. 19), and Jacob's conditional vow (vv. 20–22) are all clearly visible in the center and left columns. A parashah setumah marker (ס) is visible after the close of the vow (v. 22), before the chapter transition to Genesis 29. The text of vv. 20–22 is particularly well preserved, and the cognate figure עַשֵּׂר אֲעַשְּׂרֶנּוּ ("tithing I will tithe it") in v. 22 is fully legible with its dagesh and sheva markings intact.

Codex Vaticanus page 24 (online viewer 0028, GEN_028_vaticanus_page_001.jpg) carries the header *CAP. XXXI* at top center — the Greek chapter division. Three columns of uncial script carry the LXX rendering of Genesis 27:30ff. through approximately Genesis 28:15–16. The uncial script is clearly legible. Page 25 (online viewer 0029, GEN_028_vaticanus_page_002.jpg) carries the continuation through Genesis 28's close and into Genesis 29. The Greek equivalents of the key Hebrew terms — *klimax* for sullam, *stēlē* for massebah, *Baithēl* for Bethel — are all legible in their respective verses.

---

**28:1 — "Isaac called to Jacob and blessed him and commanded him":**
The sequence of three verbs — *wayyiqra'* (called), *wayĕvārekh* (blessed), *wayĕṣawwēhū* (commanded) — is unusual. The blessing and the command are simultaneous acts: Isaac does not merely give instructions but wraps them in the formal act of blessing. The verb *ṣāwāh* (צָוָה, "to command, to charge") elevates the prohibition against Canaanite wives from parental advice to a formal charge — a near-legal directive with the weight of the patriarchal blessing behind it. The LXX Vaticanus reads *proskalēsamenos de Isaak ton Iakōb eulogēsen auton kai eneteilato autō* (καλεσάμενος δὲ Ισαακ τὸν Ιακωβ εὐλόγησεν αὐτὸν καὶ ἐνετείλατο αὐτῷ) — accurately preserving all three verbs: called, blessed, charged.

---

**28:3 — El Shaddai (אֵל שַׁדַּי):**
The divine epithet *'El Shaddai* is preserved untranslated per project convention. This title appears seven times in the Pentateuch (Gen 17:1; 28:3; 35:11; 43:14; 48:3; Exod 6:3; Num 24:4) and is the distinctive name associated with the patriarchal period. Its etymology is debated: the most common derivations are from *shadad* ("the almighty / destroyer"), *shad* ("mountain / the mountain one"), Akkadian *šadû* ("mountain"), or *shaddayim* ("breasts / nurturing one" — the sustainer). The meaning "Almighty" in English translations stems from the LXX rendering *pantokratōr* (παντοκράτωρ, "all-powerful / omnipotent") and the Vulgate's *omnipotens*. EMET preserves the Hebrew form untranslated. In Vaticanus the parallel here reads *ho theos mou eulogēsai se* — the standard LXX handling of the blessing formula, with *theos* for God.

---

**28:4 — "The land of your sojournings" (*'erets mĕgurêkhā*, אֶרֶץ מְגֻרֶיךָ):**
The noun *mĕgurîm* (מְגוּרִים, "sojournings, temporary dwelling places, places where one is a stranger/ger") is from the root *gûr* (גּוּר, "to sojourn, to dwell as a foreigner, to be a temporary resident"). The phrase *'erets mĕgurêkhā* — "the land of your sojournings" — is a technical designation acknowledging that the patriarchs did not fully own the land but were resident aliens (*gēr*) in Canaan. The promise conveys that the land where they sojourned as foreigners will become their own possession (*rĕshut*). EMET renders *mĕgurîm* as "sojournings" preserving the root sense of alien temporary residence. The LXX Vaticanus reads *tēn gēn en hē paroikeis* (τὴν γῆν ἐν ᾗ παροικεῖς) — "the land in which you sojourn" — using *paroikeō* ("to dwell alongside as a foreigner"), exactly cognate to the Hebrew.

---

**28:6–8 — The Esau Interlude: Narrative Function:**
The brief Esau digression (vv. 6–9) interrupts what is otherwise a direct narrative line from Isaac's charge (vv. 1–5) to Jacob's departure (v. 10). It serves two purposes: (1) it creates narrative irony — Esau's attempt to please his father by taking a "better" wife (from Ishmael's line, thus still within the broader Abrahamic family) actually highlights his continued failure to understand what his father values; (2) it anchors the Ishmaelite genealogy (Mahalath/Basemath, daughter of Ishmael, sister of Nebaioth) within the main narrative. The name given here as *Maḥalat* (מָחֲלַת) differs from Genesis 36:3, where Esau's Ishmaelite wife is called *Bāśĕmat* (בָּשְׂמַת, "Basemath"). This is a recognized textual difficulty (possibly different names for the same woman, or a transmission variant). EMET renders both names as they appear in their respective verses without harmonizing.

---

**28:10 — "And Jacob went out from Beer-sheba and went toward Haran":**
The verse is notable for what it does not say. There is no emotional commentary, no farewell scene rendered, no description of Jacob's state of mind. The narrator gives only two bare verbs of motion: *wayyēṣē'* (went out) and *wayyēlekh* (went). This is characteristic of the Hebrew narrative economy that we saw operating throughout Genesis 27. The contrast with the richly dramatized scenes of chapters 27 and 29 frames the solitude of 28:10–22 as a liminal space — the journey between the household Jacob has fled and the household he is journeying toward. The LXX Vaticanus reads *exēlthen de Iakōb apo Phreatos tou horkou kai eporeuthe eis Charran* (ἐξῆλθεν δὲ Ιακωβ ἀπὸ τοῦ Φρέατος τοῦ ὅρκου καὶ ἐπορεύθη εἰς Χαρραν) — "Jacob went out from the Well of the Oath and journeyed to Haran" — rendering *Be'er Sheva* (בְּאֵר שֶׁבַע) as *Phrear tou horkou* ("Well of the Oath"), its etymological sense (see Gen 21:31, 26:33).

---

**28:11 — "He came upon a place" (*wayyifga' bammakom*, וַיִּפְגַּע בַּמָּקוֹם):**
The verb *pāga'* (פָּגַע) is typically rendered "came to" or "came upon" but carries a richer meaning: "to encounter, to light upon, to meet by happening, to fall upon." It is used for chance encounters, for prayer (hitting upon God — cf. Jer 7:16, "Do not pray for this people or lift up cry or prayer for them or make intercession [*al-tifga'*] to me"), and for hostile encounters. Here the use of *pāga'* for Jacob's arrival at the unnamed *māqōm* (place) suggests that Jacob *happened upon* this location rather than having chosen it as a campsite — the encounter was unplanned, undesigned, and thereby all the more theologically significant. The *māqōm* (הַמָּקוֹם) later becomes a Jewish circumlocution for God himself (*HaMaqom* — the Place), rooted partly in this very theophany. EMET renders *pāga'* as "came upon" to preserve the sense of happening/encounter over deliberate arrival. The LXX Vaticanus reads *kai apēntēsen topō* (καὶ ἀπήντησεν τόπῳ) — "and he met/encountered a place" — *apantaō* being a meeting/encounter verb, exactly parallel to *pāga'*.

**28:11 — "His head-rest" (*mĕra'ăshōtāyw*, מְרַאֲשֹׁתָיו):**
The noun *mĕra'ăshōt* is a locative plural meaning "the place(s) at [one's] head" — a head-rest position, not necessarily a single stone used as a pillow. The text says Jacob took "from the stones of the place" (*mē'avnê hammāqōm*) — plural stones — and placed them as his head-rest. Some read this as one stone selected from many; others as multiple stones arranged at his head. The same word recurs in v. 18 (the stone he set up as a massebah). EMET renders it "head-rest" preserving the unusual locative construction rather than the smoother "under his head."

---

**28:12 — sullam (סֻלָּם) — hapax legomenon:**
The word *sullam* (סֻלָּם) is a hapax legomenon — it appears only here in the entire Hebrew Bible, making it one of the most debated vocabulary items in Genesis. The traditional rendering is "ladder," derived from the root *sālal* (סָלַל, "to heap up, to cast up, to build a road or ramp by piling earth"). However, the cognate Akkadian *simmiltu* / *simmillu* refers specifically to a staircase or stairway, and the Mesopotamian architectural context — particularly the ziggurats of Mesopotamia with their external stairways/ramps connecting earth to an elevated temple-platform — makes "stairway" or "ramp" at least as plausible as "ladder." The vision fits the ziggurat-model: a massive earthwork structure (*muṣṣāb 'arṣāh* — "set/founded on the earth") whose top (*rō'shō*) reaches the heavens, with divine beings (messengers/angels) moving up and down its stairway. The etymology of the name Babylon (Babel — "gate of God") and the Bethel naming (v. 17: "gate of heaven") both fit this ziggurat template. EMET renders *sullam* as "a stairway/ramp" with the Hebrew preserved in the text and this note on the hapax. The LXX Vaticanus reads *klimax* (κλίμαξ) — literally "ladder, stairway, steps" — a Greek word that encompasses both ladder and staircase and is etymologically related to *klinō* (to lean/incline), appropriate for either a leaning ladder or a ramp.

**28:12 — "Ascending and descending" (*'olim wĕyōrĕdim*, עֹלִים וְיֹרְדִים) — ascending first:**
The manifest note flags this correctly: the messengers are ascending *first*, then descending. This is theologically striking if the vision is interpreted as messengers active in the earthly realm who return to heaven and then come back down — i.e., they were already on the earth when Jacob encountered the place. Alternatively, the ascent-first sequence could indicate that the angels serving as Jacob's guardians on earth were now ascending to report to God before receiving new instructions to descend with the divine promise that follows in v. 13. The ascending-first sequence is unique among similar heavenly-vision texts (contrast John 1:51, where Jesus reverses the order, perhaps intentionally: "ascending and descending *upon the Son of Man*" — echoing but reversing this verse). EMET preserves the exact order of the Hebrew: *ascending and descending*.

---

**28:13 — 'alav (עָלָיו) — the critical ambiguity:**
This is the key textual ambiguity of the chapter. The verse reads: *wĕhinnēh YHWH niṣṣāv 'ālāyw* — "and behold, the LORD stood *'ālāyw*." The preposition *'al* with third-person masculine singular suffix (*'ālāyw*) can mean:

1. **"upon it" / "above it"** — i.e., the LORD stood atop the *sullam* (the stairway/ladder), at the heavenly end of it, with *'ālāyw* referring back to the *sullam* (grammatically masculine).
2. **"beside him" / "over him"** — i.e., the LORD stood beside/over Jacob himself, with *'ālāyw* referring to Jacob (also grammatically masculine, and the nearest antecedent person).

Most ancient versions support reading 1: the LXX Vaticanus reads *epestēriktai ep' autēs* (ἐπεστήρικται ἐπ' αὐτῆς) — "was standing upon it" — using the *feminine* form *autēs* for the ladder/stairway (*klimax*, feminine in Greek), distinctly indicating the ladder rather than Jacob. The Vulgate reads *innixus scalae* ("leaning upon the ladder"). Rabbinic tradition (e.g., Rashi citing Bereshit Rabbah 69:3) is split: some read "above it" (the ladder), emphasizing God's transcendence above the whole cosmic structure; others prefer "beside him" (Jacob), making it a direct personal theophany. EMET renders the Hebrew literally as *'alav* ("above it / beside him") and places the ambiguity in brackets: "[upon it / beside him]" — preserving the unresolved syntax of the source. The weight of the ancient versions favors "upon it" (the stairway), but the narrative intimacy of what follows (God speaking directly to Jacob, promising to be with *him* personally) fits "beside him" equally well.

---

**28:14 — "Shall bless themselves" (*wĕnivrĕkhū*, וְנִבְרְכוּ) — the hitpa'el / niphal ambiguity:**
The verb here is *nivrĕkhū* (Niphal of *bārak*) — either "they shall be blessed" (passive: all families will receive blessing through you) or reflexive "they shall bless themselves" (niphal-reflexive: all families will invoke your name in self-blessing, i.e., "may I be like Jacob"). The identical ambiguity appears in the Abrahamic covenant promise of Genesis 12:3 (*wĕnivrĕkhū bĕkhā kol mishpĕḥōt hā'ădāmāh*) and 18:18. At 22:18 and 26:4, the Hitpa'el (*wĕhitbārăkhū*) is used instead, which more strongly supports the reflexive reading. Both interpretations have significant theological weight: the passive reading (all nations will *receive* blessing through the Abrahamic line) underlies Paul's argument in Galatians 3:8; the reflexive reading (all nations will *invoke* the Abrahamic blessing as a byword for good fortune) is closer to the grammatical force of the Niphal. EMET renders it "shall bless themselves" (the reflexive-niphal) with this note, following the Hebrew grammatical form.

---

**28:15 — "I will bring you back to this land" (*hă'ădāmāh*, הָאֲדָמָה vs. *hā'āreṣ*, הָאָרֶץ):**
The term used here for "land" is *hā'ădāmāh* ("the ground / the soil / the earth as arable land") rather than *hā'āreṣ* ("the land / the territory / the country") used in v. 13. Both appear in close proximity in this passage. *'ădāmāh* (from *'ādam* — humanity, red earth) carries the sense of soil and earthly substance, and may be used here to emphasize the physical, grounded reality of the promise — Jacob will return to this *very ground* — while *'āreṣ* in v. 13 refers more broadly to the territory. EMET renders *'ădāmāh* as "land" but notes the distinction from v. 13's *'āreṣ*.

**28:15 — "Until I have done what I have spoken to you" — the unusual qatal:**
The close of v. 15 uses *'āśîtî* (עָשִׂיתִי, perfect/qatal of *'āśāh* — "I have done") rather than the expected imperfect "until I do." The use of the perfect/complete tense (*'āśîtî*) for a future action — "until I have done [what I have spoken]" — is the prophetic perfect: the action is spoken of as certain, already-accomplished in the divine perspective. This is grammatically and theologically significant: God's completion of the promise is treated as assured from the moment of the oath. EMET renders it "until I have done" (qatal rendered as English perfect) to capture this unusual form, rather than smoothing to the future "until I do."

---

**28:16 — "Surely the LORD is in this place, and I — I did not know":**
The Hebrew reads *'ākhēn yēsh YHWH bammāqōm hazzeh wĕ'ānōkhî lō' yāda'tî* (אָכֵן יֵשׁ יְהוָה בַּמָּקוֹם הַזֶּה וְאָנֹכִי לֹא יָדַעְתִּי). Several features demand attention:

1. *'ākhēn* (אָכֵן, "surely, truly, indeed") — this particle of affirmation occurs in startled recognition contexts elsewhere (e.g., Exod 2:14: *'ākhēn nōda' haddāvār* — "truly the matter is known"). Jacob uses it as an exclamation of discovery: "indeed! truly!"

2. *yēsh YHWH* — the use of the existential particle *yēsh* (יֵשׁ, "there is / it is") with the divine name is striking. Jacob is not saying "God was here" but "God *is* here" — present-tense existential assertion about the place. The holiness is intrinsic to the location.

3. *wĕ'ānōkhî lō' yāda'tî* — "and I — I did not know." The doubled first-person (*wĕ'ānōkhî*... the emphasis of the pronoun before the verb) throws the ignorance into relief: "but *I*, for my part, did not know." This is one of the Bible's great recognition speeches — a moment of profound awareness of divine presence in an unexpected place. EMET preserves the doubled pronoun: "and I — I did not know."

---

**28:17 — "House of God" and "gate of heaven" — bet-El and sha'ar ha-shamayim:**
Jacob identifies the *māqōm* with two formulas: *bêt 'ĕlōhîm* (בֵּית אֱלֹהִים, "house of God") and *sha'ar hashshāmāyim* (שַׁעַר הַשָּׁמַיִם, "gate of the heavens"). The first formula becomes the name of the site (v. 19: *bêt-'ēl*). The second formula — "gate of heaven" — echoes the Babylonian etymology of *Bāb-ili* ("gate of God"), and connects the *sullam*-vision directly to the ancient Near Eastern architectural and theological concept of the temple as the point where heaven and earth meet. The ziggurat of Babylon (Babel) in Genesis 11 was the human attempt to construct such a gate; here at Bethel, God himself establishes the connection without human engineering. The inversion is pointed: Babel's builders sought to *reach* heaven by human effort; Jacob *stumbles upon* the place where heaven touches earth without seeking it. The LXX Vaticanus renders *bêt 'ĕlōhîm* as *oikos theou* (οἶκος θεοῦ, "house of God") and *sha'ar hashshāmāyim* as *pylē tou ouranou* (πύλη τοῦ οὐρανοῦ, "gate of heaven") — exact and elegant renderings.

---

**28:18 — massebah (מַצֵּבָה) — standing-stone/pillar:**
The word *maṣṣēvāh* (מַצֵּבָה) is from the root *nāṣav* (נָצַב, "to stand, to set upright, to plant in the ground") — same root used for the *sullam* being "set" (*muṣṣāv*) in v. 12. The *maṣṣēvāh* is a standing stone — a single upright stone set in the ground as a memorial, a boundary marker, or a sacred stele. In the later Deuteronomic law (Deut 16:22) the *maṣṣēvāh* is condemned as a Canaanite cultic object — "you shall not set up for yourself a *maṣṣēvāh* which the LORD your God hates." The use of the same term here for Jacob's act shows that the patriarchal period predates the Deuteronomic prohibition: what Jacob does is not yet categorized as idolatrous. The anointing of the stone with oil (*wayyiṣōq shemen 'al-rō'shāh*) is an act of consecration — it is designated as holy, as *God's* stone, not a fertility idol. EMET preserves *massebah* in transliteration with a note, following the project convention for culturally irreducible terms. The LXX Vaticanus reads *stēlēn* (στήλην) — "a stele/pillar" — the standard Greek term for an upright memorial stone, accurate and equivalent.

---

**28:19 — "But the name of the city had formerly been Luz" (*wĕ'ûlām shēm hā'îr lĕrī'shōnāh lûz*, וְאוּלָם שֵׁם הָעִיר לְרִאשֹׁנָה לוּז):**
The name Luz (לוּז) is connected with the almond tree (*lûz* — almond; cf. the play on *lûz*/*shāqēd* in Jer 1:11–12), and with a city near Bethel mentioned in Judges 1:23–26 (where the Luz people are treated as distinct from the Bethelites). The narrator inserts this aetiological note to anchor the vision within geography — Jacob is not at some vague "place" (*māqōm*) but at a real location that the reader knows as Bethel. The verse thus performs double aetiological work: explaining both the name Bethel (from this episode) and the prior name Luz (from before this episode). The LXX Vaticanus renders *lûz* as *Oulammaous* (Οὐλαμμαούς) in some manuscripts but here preserves *Louza* (Λουζα), a transliteration of the Hebrew place-name.

---

**28:20–22 — Jacob's Conditional Vow: Theological Significance:**
The structure of the vow in vv. 20–22 is formally a conditional vow (*neder*): *'im... wĕ'im... wĕhāyāh YHWH lî lē'lōhîm* — "if... and if... then the LORD shall be to me for God." The conditionality has long been a subject of rabbinic and theological debate. Is Jacob bargaining with God? Is he treating the divine promises of vv. 13–15 as not yet binding on him — extracting further assurances before committing to YHWH as *his* God? Several observations:

1. The *'im* (אִם, "if") structure is a genuine condition, not a mere temporal marker. Jacob says: *if* God provides bread and clothing and safe return — *then* YHWH shall be my God. The conditionality is real.

2. This contrasts sharply with the preceding Abrahamic covenant material, where God's promises are unconditional. Jacob introduces conditionality into a relationship God has just pledged unconditionally (v. 15: "I will not leave you until I have done what I have spoken to you").

3. The vow can be read charitably: Jacob is not refusing to acknowledge God but expressing the terms by which he will recognize the fulfillment of God's promise in his experience — i.e., he is committing to worship and tithe *on condition that he survives*, which is the only sensible condition from a frightened man fleeing for his life.

4. The second half of the vow (v. 22) pledges the *massebah* as a permanent house of God and commits a tithe (*'aśśēr 'ă'aśśĕrennū*, the cognate-accusative "tithing I will tithe it") — the first mention of the tithe in the Genesis narrative, anticipating the Mosaic tithe legislation.

EMET renders the vow with maximum structural fidelity, preserving the conditional *'im* ("if") and the resultative *wĕhāyāh* ("then... shall be") without smoothing the conditionality. The LXX Vaticanus renders the vow accurately, using *ean* (ἐάν, "if") for the Hebrew *'im*, and *estai moi kyrios eis theon* (ἔσται μοι κύριος εἰς θεόν) for *wĕhāyāh YHWH lî lē'lōhîm* — "the LORD shall be to me for/as God" — preserving the formulation *eis theon* ("for a God / as God") rather than simply *theos mou* ("my God"), faithfully representing the Hebrew *lê'lōhîm*.

**28:22 — "Tithing I will tithe it" (*'aśśēr 'ă'aśśĕrennū*, עַשֵּׂר אֲעַשְּׂרֶנּוּ) — cognate absolute:**
The cognate absolute construction (*figura etymologica*) — infinitive absolute + finite verb of the same root — is the same emphatic structure as Isaac's "trembling a trembling" in 27:33. Here the root *'āśar* (עָשַׂר, "to tithe, to give a tenth") is doubled: *'aśśēr 'ă'aśśĕrennū* — literally "tithing I will tithe it." The construction emphasizes the completeness and certainty of the commitment: Jacob is not merely promising to tithe but intensifying the promise with the cognate construction. The LXX Vaticanus renders *dekatē dekatōsō* (δεκάτῃ δεκατώσω) — "with a tenth I will tithe" — preserving the cognate figure in Greek exactly as it does for other Hebrew cognate absolutes (cf. the LXX rendering of 27:33). This is one of the Vaticanus LXX's most consistent and elegant habits of translation.

---

**LXX Vaticanus Observations (Pages 24–25):**

Codex Vaticanus pages 24–25 carry Genesis 28 across two pages in three-column uncial. The header *CAP. XXXI* on page 24 reflects the ancient Greek chapter division system, distinct from the modern Hebrew/English chapter divisions. Key LXX readings and variants for Genesis 28:

1. **28:1** — LXX *proskalēsamenos de Isaak ton Iakōb* — "And calling to himself Jacob, Isaac..." — the LXX uses the aorist participle *proskalēsamenos* for the Hebrew sequential *wayyiqra'*, good formal parallel.

2. **28:3** — LXX does not reproduce *El Shaddai* as a divine name here but reads *ho theos mou* — possibly representing a variant Hebrew tradition or a theological flattening of the archaic epithet.

3. **28:10** — LXX renders *Be'er Sheva* as *Phrear tou horkou* (Φρέαρ τοῦ ὅρκου, "Well of the Oath") — etymological translation of the place-name (cf. Gen 21:31, 26:33).

4. **28:11** — LXX *apēntēsen topō* (ἀπήντησεν τόπῳ) — "met/encountered a place" — preserving the *pāga'*-encounter nuance well.

5. **28:12** — LXX *klimax* (κλίμαξ) for *sullam* (סֻלָּם) — "ladder/stairway." The choice of *klimax* (a word for stairs, a ladder, or any graduated ascent) is fitting and ambiguous in the same way as the Hebrew hapax.

6. **28:12** — LXX *hoi angeloi tou theou* (οἱ ἄγγελοι τοῦ θεοῦ) for *mal'ăkhê 'ĕlōhîm* — "messengers/angels of God" — accurate rendering; *angelos* (ἄγγελος) is the standard LXX equivalent of *mal'ākh* (מַלְאָךְ, "messenger / envoy / angel"), preserving the ambiguity between divine messengers and what later tradition calls angels.

7. **28:13** — LXX *epestēriktai ep' autēs* (ἐπεστήρικται ἐπ' αὐτῆς) — "was standing/supported upon it [feminine]" — the feminine *autēs* refers to *klimax* (ladder, feminine in Greek), resolving the Hebrew *'ālāyw* ambiguity in favor of "upon the stairway/ladder" rather than "beside Jacob." This is a significant textual witness supporting the "above the ladder" reading.

8. **28:14** — LXX *plēthunthēsontai* (πληθυνθήσονται) for *yāpaṣ* ("spread forth") — "shall be multiplied" — slightly different word choice that emphasizes numerical increase rather than geographic expansion.

9. **28:14** — LXX *eneulogēthēsontai* (ἐνευλογηθήσονται) for *wĕnivrĕkhū* — "shall be blessed in" — the LXX uses a passive (*-thēsontai*) construction here, supporting the passive reading of the Niphal ("shall be blessed") over the reflexive ("shall bless themselves").

10. **28:17** — LXX *oikos theou* (οἶκος θεοῦ) and *pylē tou ouranou* (πύλη τοῦ οὐρανοῦ) — exact equivalents, accurate.

11. **28:18** — LXX *stēlēn* (στήλην) for *maṣṣēvāh* — "a stele/pillar" — the standard and accurate LXX rendering for *maṣṣēvāh* throughout.

12. **28:19** — LXX *Baithēl* (Βαιθήλ) for *Bêt-'ēl* — the standard Greek transliteration. The prior name *Luz* is rendered *Louza* (Λουζα) — transliteration.

13. **28:20–22** — LXX preserves the conditional structure with *ean* (ἐάν, "if") — the conditionality of Jacob's vow is maintained in the LXX tradition without harmonization or softening.

14. **28:22** — LXX *dekatē dekatōsō* (δεκάτῃ δεκατώσω) — "with a tenth I will tithe" — elegant cognate construction preserving the Hebrew *figura etymologica* exactly.

**DSS Note:**
Per the chapter manifest, no significant Genesis 28 fragments from the Dead Sea Scrolls are extant. The Leningrad Codex (primary) and Codex Vaticanus LXX (secondary) are the principal manuscript witnesses examined for this chapter.
